candidates.title: Nationalist gymnastics as identity-tech precedent
candidates.shift: Turkish archival records reveal the Yugoslav Sokol movement's use of mass gymnastics/education to engineer pan-Slavic identity and unity a century ago.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: a historical template for how physical-culture movements manufacture cohesion could resurface as a model (or warning) for modern identity-engineering via fitness/wellness subcultures.
candidates.evidence: Study of Sokol organization in Yugoslavia drawn from Turkish Republican archives.

candidates.title: Scam narratives as folkloric infrastructure
candidates.shift: The 'imprisoned noble' scam story lineage (advance-fee fraud) is reframed as a durable folk narrative form that merely changes medium, not structure.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: suggests fraud narratives are a stable cultural genre; foresight work on disinformation may benefit from folklore/narrative-studies lenses rather than tech-only framing.
candidates.evidence: MIT Press Reader traces scam letter lineage from imprisoned-noble tales to email-era 419 scams.

candidates.title: Scambaiting as monetized vigilante profession
candidates.shift: Amateur anti-scam 'scambaiting' has evolved from hobby into structured, content-monetized 'entrepreneurial digilantism' on platforms like YouTube.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: a new hybrid occupation—private, profit-driven counter-fraud enforcement—raises questions about legitimacy, labor, and informal justice systems online.
candidates.evidence: UK study frames scambaiting's platform-driven professionalization as entrepreneurial digilantism.

candidates.title: Espionage training sites as heritage circuits
candidates.shift: WWII covert-ops training facilities (Camp X) are becoming preserved memorial/tourism sites, embedding intelligence tradecraft into public heritage narrative.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: as cyber and info-war anxieties rise, physical espionage heritage sites may gain new symbolic use as counterpoints/analogies for digital-era tradecraft literacy.
candidates.evidence: Atlas Obscura profile of Camp X, Allied sabotage/espionage training school in Ontario.

candidates.title: Temple overcrowding forcing quiet-site rediscovery
candidates.shift: Kyoto's famous temples are so oversaturated with tourists that lesser-known sites like Saihō-ji are being repositioned as 'true' cultural experiences.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: overtourism is pushing cultural heritage management toward deliberate obscurity/scarcity marketing—an odd inversion of preservation-via-visibility logic.
candidates.evidence: Atlas Obscura notes Kyoto's 1,600+ temples yet persistent overcrowding at famous sites, elevating Saihō-ji's appeal.

candidates.title: Vanished rail infrastructure as urban absence-marker
candidates.shift: Nottingham Victoria Station's disappearance (demolished, replaced by a shopping centre) stands as a case of erased transit infrastructure shaping city memory.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: an absence-signal — the gap left by removed infrastructure often becomes a durable cultural/political reference point in later urban planning debates.
candidates.evidence: Atlas Obscura on the vanished Nottingham Victoria Railway Station, once a major rail 'prize' site.

candidates.title: 175-year single-storefront survival as resilience case
candidates.shift: A rural New York general store has operated continuously since 1851 through wars, depressions, and pandemics under just three family owners.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: an extreme outlier of small-business longevity offers a natural experiment in what structurally insulates micro-enterprises from macro shocks over centuries.
candidates.evidence: Atlas Obscura profile of Marilla Country Store, open since 1851, three family owners.

candidates.title: Deep-sea chemosynthetic symbiosis as astrobiology analog
candidates.shift: A newly described sulfur-oxidizing endosymbiont in an Atacama Trench bivalve expands the catalogue of chemosynthetic life at extreme depth/pressure.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: each new extremophile symbiosis found on Earth incrementally sharpens biosignature models for subsurface ocean worlds (Europa, Enceladus) — slow-burn astrobiology infrastructure.
candidates.evidence: Scientific Reports describes new Vesicomyosocius atacamensis endosymbiont species from Atacama Trench depths.

candidates.title: Golden Dome space-weapon legal ambiguity
candidates.shift: Legal scholars find the US 'Golden Dome' orbital missile defense system may violate Outer Space Treaty norms absent strict human-authorization and engagement limits.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: reframes missile-defense infrastructure as a live test case for whether 'defense' orbital systems can remain treaty-compliant as autonomy in engagement increases.
candidates.evidence: Netherlands International Law Review assesses Golden Dome's legality under international space law, flags autonomy/authorization risks.

candidates.title: Cislunar supply-chain due diligence as new legal category
candidates.shift: Legal/commercial frameworks are being built for supply-chain due diligence in space manufacturing and lunar resource extraction, treating ISRU as an emerging industrial sector.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: quietly normalizes lunar/orbital extraction as a 'supply chain,' importing terrestrial ESG/due-diligence apparatus into space law well before extraction is routine.
candidates.evidence: Space and Planetary Resources journal frames due diligence frameworks for lunar ISRU and orbital servicing as near-term legal/commercial planning.

candidates.title: Space tech reframed as Global South development lever
candidates.shift: A review argues downstream geospatial/space applications are structurally driving economic prosperity in the Global South, contesting the frame of space as an 'industrialized luxury.'
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: peripheral-geography reframing — treats orbital infrastructure as development infrastructure, which could shift how foresight models space access equity.
candidates.evidence: Global South & Sustainable Development journal reviews macroeconomic impact of space applications in the Global South.

candidates.title: Community forestry vs. carbon-instrument tech capture
candidates.shift: A Cambridge report examines 'smart forest' digital monitoring tools being deployed for carbon/biodiversity metrics, and community-led alternatives resisting instrumentalization of forests as compliance tools.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: a friction point — forests reframed purely as carbon-accounting infrastructure vs. community stewardship models; could presage backlash against algorithmic environmental governance.
candidates.evidence: Cambridge Apollo 'Smart Forests Final Report' on community-led forest technologies vs. carbon/biodiversity-target-driven digitization.

candidates.title: Water-footprint accounting exposes embedded-water blind spot
candidates.shift: A Great Salt Lake case study argues standard hydrology ignores water embedded in traded food products, missing a major driver of basin depletion.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: reframing of water scarcity as a trade/accounting failure rather than pure supply problem — implications for how foresight models drought risk via virtual-water trade flows.
candidates.evidence: USU case study integrates hydrology with human water-footprint accounting for the drying Great Salt Lake Basin.

candidates.title: Cruise industry 'bluewashing' as green-criminology case
candidates.shift: Green criminologists apply an eco-justice/crime lens to cruise industry sustainability claims, framing environmental harm as a criminological rather than merely regulatory issue.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: signals a broader shift of climate-harm discourse from compliance/PR framing into criminal-justice framing — could presage litigation strategies against other 'bluewashing' sectors.
candidates.evidence: Critical Criminology journal applies green criminology/eco-justice concepts to cruise industry sustainability claims.

candidates.title: Blockchain auctions for cross-border maritime trade clearing
candidates.shift: A blockchain-driven multinational auction/resource-optimization framework (B-MARCO) proposes replacing fragmented customs/carrier clearing systems with automated cross-border matching.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: if adopted, could quietly restructure flag-of-convenience and port-authority power dynamics by disintermediating slow batch clearing with algorithmic auctions.
candidates.evidence: Frontiers in Marine Science proposes B-MARCO blockchain auction framework for cross-border maritime logistics and customs coordination.

candidates.title: AI governance patchwork as its own market friction
candidates.shift: Canadian researchers propose adapting NIST's AI Risk Management Framework to reconcile fragmented federal/provincial AI regulations across Canada.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: an early case study in cross-jurisdictional AI regulatory patchwork becoming a compliance cost center of its own — a pattern likely to replicate globally at sub-national levels.
candidates.evidence: SCITUS framework adapts NIST AI RMF across fragmented Canadian federal/provincial AI regulatory landscape.

candidates.title: Migrant self-arson reframed as political agency, not just victimhood
candidates.shift: Scholars analyze the #FreeTheMoria6 campaign's narrative battle over whether migrants who set fire to the Moria camp are agents of resistance or criminals.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: highlights an underexplored discursive frontier — migrant-led disruptive acts being reframed as political speech, with second-order implications for asylum/criminal law narratives.
candidates.evidence: Journal of Ethnic and Migration Studies examines framing contests over migrant agency in the #FreeTheMoria6 campaign.
candidates.source: Journal of Ethnic and Migration Studies

candidates.title: Offshore wind's legal gridlock as property-rights redesign case
candidates.shift: Legal scholars propose reworking property and liability rules to unblock offshore wind development bottlenecks tied to grid access and permitting.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: an early test bed for how legal-institutional redesign (not just tech) may be the binding constraint on marine renewable buildout — a template transferable to other ocean-use conflicts.
candidates.evidence: Columbia Business Law Review proposes property/liability rule reforms to resolve offshore wind's 'gridlock economy.'

candidates.title: Post-quantum identity infrastructure quietly being rebuilt
candidates.shift: New multivariate-cryptography anonymous certificate schemes are emerging ahead of NIST's 2030 ECC deprecation deadline for post-quantum migration.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: unglamorous but foundational — the entire web-trust/identity stack is being re-engineered under quantum threat, with anonymity properties as a secondary design goal worth tracking.
candidates.evidence: arXiv paper proposes multivariate-crypto anonymous certificate scheme ahead of NIST's ECC deprecation by 2030.

candidates.title: Leakage-resistant crypto for constrained hardware, quietly hardening IoT
candidates.shift: New authenticated-encryption constructions target single-length-key block ciphers to resist side-channel leakage on lightweight/embedded devices.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: incremental but signals crypto-hardening is trickling down to the cheapest, most numerous devices (IoT/industrial sensors) — the actual attack surface of the future.
candidates.evidence: IACR TCHES paper presents UEDTDM/UEDTMX leakage-resistant AEAD constructions for single-length-key block ciphers.

candidates.title: Customary international law as narrative construction, not just practice
candidates.shift: Legal theorists argue the ILC's formal doctrine on customary international law formation obscures that custom is actually built through contested narrative-making.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: reframes 'customary law' — the backbone for space, maritime, and AI governance gaps — as a rhetorical battlefield rather than neutral accretion of state practice.
candidates.evidence: Leiden Journal of International Law critiques ILC Draft Conclusions on customary law identification as narrative construction.

candidates.title: Deepfake fraud outpacing fragmented legal response
candidates.shift: Analysts argue 'Fraud-as-a-Service' deepfake schemes have outpaced fragmented global regulation, proposing an enforced multi-layered technical mandate for biometric integrity.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative(Trend): a now-familiar governance-lag story, but the 'Fraud-as-a-Service' commercialization angle is worth tracking as an industrialized crime-as-platform pattern.
candidates.evidence: Computer Law & Security Review proposes multi-layered governance mandate against deepfake Fraud-as-a-Service and biometric fraud.

candidates.title: Biometric multimodality as security-theater arms race
candidates.shift: Review of soft-computing techniques for biometric authentication highlights persistent spoofing/failure-to-enrol problems despite multimodal fusion approaches.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative(Trend): standard incremental security research, but the persistent unimodal failure modes suggest biometric infrastructure remains more brittle than public trust in it assumes.
candidates.evidence: Archives of Computational Methods in Engineering reviews soft-computing biometric authentication challenges including spoofing and failure-to-enrol.
candidates.source: Archives of Computational Methods in Engineering

candidates.title: Disinformation research critiqued via digital-colonialism lens
candidates.shift: A meta-review argues existing social-science literature on deepfakes/disinformation needs reframing through digital-colonialism critique, not just information-disorder theory.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: a reframing signal — shifts blame/agency analysis of disinformation from purely technical failure to geopolitical power asymmetry, which could redirect policy funding priorities.
candidates.evidence: Paper critiques disinformation/deepfake research through integration of information-disorder theory and digital-colonialism critique.

candidates.title: Open-source power-grid modeling tools go grassroots
candidates.shift: A hobbyist-launched open-source web UI for power-flow/grid modeling (BambooGrid) suggests grid-planning tools are becoming accessible outside utility/engineering firms.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: democratized grid-modeling tooling could enable community/activist groups to contest utility infrastructure plans with their own technical modeling — a shift in whose analysis counts.
candidates.evidence: Hacker News Show HN post for BambooGrid, open-source web UI for power grid modeling and power flow, gaining modest traction.

candidates.title: Federated learning + blockchain anchoring for patient monitoring
candidates.shift: A privacy-preserving patient-monitoring framework combines federated learning with lightweight blockchain anchoring instead of centralizing raw medical records.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative(Trend): standard convergence pattern (federated ML + blockchain) applied to health data — worth tracking mainly for how 'lightweight anchoring' claims hold up at scale.
candidates.evidence: Sci journal proposes federated learning framework with blockchain anchoring for privacy-preserving patient monitoring.

candidates.title: Capital-compute market still structurally missing
candidates.shift: Commentary argues the US has unmatched capital and compute demand but still lacks a functioning market mechanism to directly connect the two.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative: names an absence — no liquid market instrument ties capital allocation to compute capacity, which could presage novel financial instruments (compute futures, compute-backed bonds).
candidates.evidence: Palladium essay argues US lacks a market wiring capital directly to compute despite having the largest reserves of both.

candidates.title: Shared-memory layer pitched as team infrastructure for human-AI work
candidates.shift: A Show HN product (ContextVault) proposes a persistent shared-memory layer between AI tools and human teams, treating institutional memory as an explicit product category.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative(Hype): small early-stage tool, but signals a coming market for 'organizational memory as a service' distinct from individual AI assistants.
candidates.evidence: Hacker News Show HN launch of ContextVault, a shared memory layer for AI and teams, 12 points/11 comments.

candidates.title: Self-hosted 'bring your own language' trading-bot runtime
candidates.shift: An open-source, self-hosted runtime lets developers deploy algorithmic trading bots in any programming language, lowering the barrier to personal quant infrastructure.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative(Hype): a small niche tool, but part of a broader trend of retail/DIY financial automation infrastructure becoming trivially self-hostable.
candidates.evidence: Hacker News Show HN launch of 'the0,' a self-hosted, language-agnostic trading bot runtime.

candidates.title: Generic AI-coevolution grand-theory papers proliferating
candidates.shift: A speculative paper claims to explain and predict ICT/AI/Web coevolution via 'four independent dimensions' of human connectivity and coordination.
candidates.ai_read: Tentative(Hype): representative of a genre of totalizing tech-history theory papers with little falsifiability — useful mainly as a marker of current futurist rhetoric style, not a signal itself.
candidates.evidence: Qeios paper proposes a four-dimensional framework explaining/predicting ICT, AI, and Web coevolution historically and predictively.
